Phenomenology & the Brentano School

Max Scheler

1874 – 1928

Value-feeling, personhood, and the emotional a priori.

Scheler does not give a “philosophy of mind” in the contemporary analytic sense. He gives something more unruly and, for this question, more fertile: a phenomenological anthropology of embodied life, affective intentionality, value, personhood, spirit, and the religious-metaphysical horizon of consciousness. The central reconstruction is this:

Scheler is anti-Cartesian about body and soul, anti-reductionist about mind and value, anti-intellectualist about cognition, and anti-solipsistic about other persons. At the level of life, body and soul are not two substances but two sides of one psychophysical process. At the level of the human person, however, there is a new irreducible principle: Geist, “spirit,” not as a ghostly substance but as objectivity, self-consciousness, value-disclosure, world-openness, and the capacity to say “no” to mere actuality. Yet spirit is powerless without life; it must be energized by drives, affect, embodiment, and sublimation. The person is not a soul-substance, not an ego, not a mind-container, and not merely a psyche; the person is the non-objectifiable unity, order, and style of acts, ultimately disclosed through love. That is the core of Scheler’s philosophy of mind.

A textual caution is important. The easily accessible German text of Die Stellung des Menschen im Kosmos often follows the 1947 edition, while Italian editorial material for Guido Cusinato’s edition stresses that the commonly known text had been reworked after Scheler’s death and that the Italian edition follows the original 1928 version. This matters especially for late claims about Geist, Drang, God, and dualism, so the strongest reconstruction should be edition-critical rather than relying on a single version of the late text.

1. Why Scheler’s “mind” cannot be isolated from love, value, and theology

Scheler’s starting point is not the isolated subject asking how it represents the world. Philosophy, for him, is a “loving participation” in the being of things; knowledge is not primarily construction but discovery and participation, and affective openness is a precondition of knowledge. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy summarizes the point clearly: Scheler treats knowledge as a relation between beings, one in which a being participates in what another is in itself, and he follows Augustine in making emotional and affective life foundational for knowledge.

This is already a philosophy of mind, but not one centered on representation. Mind is not first a picture-making faculty; it is an affectively opened relation to value-laden reality. The world is given before it is theorized. Love opens the world. This is why theology is not an external appendage in Scheler: the human capacity for world-openness, value-disclosure, and love tends toward the infinite and the absolute. Scheler’s own formula, as reported in the SEP entry, is that knowledge is ultimately “from the divine and for the divine.”

Italian scholarship and editorial reception often captures this better than Anglophone reception does. Vittorio d’Anna’s edition of Scheler’s Fenomenologia e teoria della conoscenza and Ordo amoris describes the first essay as freeing phenomenological experience from consciousness and the second as discovering a rigorous logic of emotional lived experience; it also states that Scheler places love at the head of knowledge in order to preserve object and spontaneity, world and life together.

So the mind, for Scheler, is not merely “consciousness.” It is structured by love, value, bodily life, pre-reflective affect, self-transcendence, and relation to the holy.

2. The basic map: life, psyche, consciousness, spirit, person

Scheler’s terms need to be kept distinct.

“Psyche” or “soul” in Scheler’s later anthropology does not mean an immaterial Cartesian substance. It means the inner side of life. Scheler explicitly says that the boundary of the psychical coincides with the boundary of the living: living beings are not only objects for external observation; they possess an inwardness, a being-for-themselves. The lowest psychical level is Gefühlsdrang, affective urge or feeling-drive, which is unconscious and lacks sensation and representation. He even attributes this lowest psychical inwardness to plants, while denying that plants have sensation or consciousness.

“Consciousness” is narrower than psyche. A being can have inwardness without consciousness. Plants, in Scheler’s model, have psychical inwardness but not consciousness. Animals have consciousness, bodily self-feedback, and a Leibschema, a body-schema, but not self-consciousness. Humans, through spirit, can objectify their own body, psyche, drives, sensations, and experiences. Scheler states this sharply: the animal has consciousness but not self-consciousness; it hears and sees without knowing that it hears and sees.

“Spirit” is not simply intelligence. Scheler argues that animals already have forms of practical intelligence. Human uniqueness therefore cannot consist merely in more intelligence. The new principle is Geist: a principle outside life in the narrow biological sense, including idea-thinking, essence-intuition, free decision, and higher emotional-volitional acts such as goodness, love, repentance, reverence, spiritual wonder, blessedness, and despair. The finite act-center in which spirit appears is the person.

“Person” is not an ego or a mind-substance. Scheler describes the person as the concrete unity of acts, a distinctive style of loving and meaning-giving, and insists that the person is non-objectifiable and not reducible to subject, ego, mind, or psyche.

The resulting ontology is stratified: inorganic nature; living psychophysical inwardness; animal consciousness; human self-consciousness; spirit; person. But the higher layers do not abolish the lower ones. Human mind is not less embodied because it is spiritual; it is a meeting point of life and spirit.

3. Scheler’s anti-Cartesian body-soul theory

Scheler’s body-soul theory is one of the strongest parts of his philosophy of mind. He rejects the Cartesian picture in which mind is a thinking substance and body a mechanism. He criticizes Descartes for treating the psychical as consciousness and for binding it too narrowly to the cerebral cortex. Scheler instead emphasizes affectivity, drive-life, bodily process, and the whole organism as the physiological field of psychic events.

His positive view is not interactionist dualism. One and the same life has, from within, psychic form and, from without, bodily form. That is, body and soul are not two ontic substances glued together. They are two modes of access to the same living process. Scheler therefore claims that the old body-soul problem has lost its metaphysical rank. The deeper contrast is not body versus soul, but spirit versus life.

This gives us a powerful extracted doctrine:

At the psychophysical level, Scheler is an aspect-dualist or identity theorist of life: the same living process is inwardly psychic and outwardly bodily. But at the anthropological-metaphysical level, he is a non-Cartesian dualist of life and spirit. The soul is not an immaterial substance opposed to the body; the soul belongs to life. Spirit is the irreducible power of objectification, essence-intuition, value-disclosure, self-consciousness, and world-openness.

This is why Scheler can deny a special human soul-substance while still rejecting naturalism. He says that the greater unity of human psychic life does not require positing a special Seelensubstanz, a soul-substance, in the human being. Yet he also insists that spirit cannot be reduced to psyche or biological evolution.

4. Consciousness: not the ground of mind, but a stratified achievement

Scheler’s theory of consciousness is strikingly non-Cartesian. Consciousness is not the original fact from which philosophy begins. It is a late and stratified phenomenon within life.

The most primitive psychical level, Gefühlsdrang, is unconscious, without sensation and representation. Reality is first encountered not as a neutral object of perception but as resistance to drive. In Die Stellung des Menschen im Kosmos, Scheler argues that the experience of reality does not come from a special sensation, perception, memory, or thought. What gives Dasein, the “there-being” or reality of things, is the experience of resistance encountered by striving, drive-laden life. This reality-experience precedes representation, perception, and consciousness.

This is an important doctrine for a comprehensive Schelerian philosophy of mind. The mind’s most basic relation to reality is not representational but conative-affective. We do not first form an inner image and then infer reality; rather, reality presses back upon life. The world is originally “there” as resistance, obstacle, pressure, solicitation, attraction, and repulsion. Conscious perception is a later articulation of a more primitive affective encounter with reality.

Scheler’s view also anticipates later embodied and enactive approaches, though it should not be collapsed into them. Perception is not passive sensory registration. It depends on drive, movement, attention, resistance, and bodily orientation. He even links primitive spatiality to movement-possibility and bodily action before explicit conscious sensation.

Self-consciousness is more advanced still. The animal, for Scheler, has consciousness and body-schema; it is “returned to itself” through sensorimotor feedback. But it remains bound to its Umwelt, its drive-correlated environment. The human being, by contrast, can take both the world and its own psychophysical life as objects. The human can objectify its body, psyche, sensations, drives, and even its own acts of knowing. This “second reflection” is the birth of self-consciousness.

5. Animal mind and human mind: intelligence is not the decisive difference

Scheler’s human-animal distinction is often misunderstood. He does not define humanity by intelligence. On the contrary, he argues that practical intelligence exists in animals and that between a clever chimpanzee and Edison considered merely as technician there is a large but still gradual difference. The specifically human difference appears only when we leave the scale of practical intelligence and reach spirit.

Animal life is bound to Umwelt. The animal does not encounter neutral objects as such; it encounters resistance-centers, attractions, and repulsions relative to its drives. Its world is functionally closed around needs, instinct, practical intelligence, and sensory-motor relevance. Human spirit breaks this closure. The human being can convert environmental resistance-centers into objects, attend to their “whatness,” and comport itself toward a world rather than merely an environment. Scheler calls this Weltoffenheit, world-openness.

The human drama therefore has a different structure. Animal behavior begins from bodily-psychic state and drive; human spiritual behavior can be motivated by the pure “suchness” of an object, independently of immediate drive-state. The person can inhibit or release a drive from a center of decision. This does not mean the person is disembodied; it means that embodiment is no longer closed within instinctual immediacy.

A Schelerian philosophy of mind would therefore reject any account that treats intelligence, tool-use, problem-solving, language-like signaling, or adaptive behavior as sufficient for personhood. Those are part of psychophysical life and practical cognition. Personhood begins where objectivity, self-consciousness, value-responsiveness, and world-openness appear.

6. Spirit: objectivity, essence, value, self-consciousness, world-openness

Scheler’s Geist is not “mind” in the everyday English sense. It is not merely intellect and not merely consciousness. It includes cognition, but also emotional and volitional acts. It includes reason, but it is broader than reason. It includes love, reverence, repentance, free decision, and essence-intuition.

The SEP gives a helpful fourfold summary of spirit in Scheler’s philosophical anthropology: spirit objectifies; spirit discloses value; spirit is self-conscious; and spirit is world-open. A merely living being relates to things through drive-satisfaction, but a spiritual being can grasp an object as what it is. Spirit is also value-disclosing, motivated by the good rather than merely by vital fulfillment; spirit can take itself as object of reflection; and spirit can wonder about being, time, death, and the meaning of existence.

The spiritual act par excellence is ideation, Ideierung. Scheler distinguishes this from technical intelligence. Technical intelligence asks how a pain arose and how it can be removed. Ideation asks what pain is, what its essence is, and what kind of world makes pain possible. In the same way, mathematics can detach number from perceived groups of things. This gives a priori insight, not by Kantian formal construction but through essence-intuition.

The human spirit can also “de-realize” the world. That does not mean denying existence in a merely theoretical judgment. It means suspending the felt pressure of actuality, the resistance-character of the real, so that essence can be grasped. Scheler connects this to Plato, Buddha, and Husserl’s reduction, though he disagrees with Husserl’s exact formulation. The human being is, in Scheler’s famous formulation, the one who can say “no” to mere reality, the ascetic of life.

The crucial point is that spirit is not generated by repression or sublimation. Scheler rejects both the classical theory, which gives spirit original causal omnipotence, and the negative theory, which derives spirit from repression. His own view is that spirit has its own essence and laws, but no original energy of its own. Sublimation supplies spirit with energy and manifestability; it does not create spirit.

This is one of the most difficult parts of Scheler’s philosophy of mind. Spirit is autonomous in meaning but dependent in energy. It is not caused by life, yet it cannot act without life. Life realizes spirit; spirit ideates and guides life. The mind is therefore neither reducible to drives nor independent of them.

7. Agency: free inhibition, sublimation, and indirect mastery of drives

Scheler’s account of agency is subtle. He does not think freedom is brute mental force conquering bodily drives. Spirit does not directly manufacture energy. Drives supply energy; spirit redirects, inhibits, organizes, and sublimates.

The human being can suspend a drive by depriving it of perceptual and imaginative nourishment, and can redirect drive-energy into spiritual acts. In Die Stellung, Scheler says the human being can withhold food from impulses by denying them perception-images and representations, and that through this constitutive “no” the human being can sublimate drive-energy into spiritual activity.

This has a strong psychological implication. The free person does not become free by abolishing affect, desire, or embodiment. Freedom is a mediated reconfiguration of affective and conative energy under value-guided spiritual direction. This is close to what we might now call attention-regulation, motivational redirection, and value-governed self-formation, but Scheler’s own language is phenomenological and metaphysical rather than clinical.

The same point explains why Scheler rejects both crude naturalism and abstract idealism. Naturalism sees the power of drives but cannot explain the autonomy of values, essences, and spiritual meaning. Idealism sees spirit but falsely imagines it has its own self-sufficient causal power. Scheler’s position is the interdependence of powerless spirit and powerful life.

8. Emotion and value: affective intentionality, not subjectivism

Scheler’s most important contribution to philosophy of mind may be his theory of emotional intentionality. Emotions are not merely inner states, subjective colorings, or irrational disturbances. Higher feeling-acts disclose values. Values are given through feeling, just as colors are given through seeing. The world is value-laden before it is theoretically known.

This does not make value subjective. Scheler is a value realist. Values are not invented by emotion; they are disclosed in emotional acts. But emotional disclosure can be distorted. Love and hate found value-consciousness: love deepens the value of a person or thing and opens higher possibilities; hate degrades and narrows. Every experience involves a preference-ordering, an ethos.

This is the doctrine of ordo amoris, the order of love. It is central for extracting a philosophy of mind. The mind is not fundamentally an “ens cogitans,” a thinking thing; it is an “ens amans,” a loving being. Italian discussions often foreground this. Anna Piazza’s Italian study states that, for Scheler, the personal or descriptive ordo amoris is the ultimate source of moral acts and action, and that it determines not only what a person can know and will but also how the deep center of spiritual being is structured over time through love and hate.

The Spanish reception makes a complementary point. Marcelo Chaparro Veas argues that human affective responses to persons and things are constitutive of moral judgments, and he distinguishes an objective or normative ordo amoris from a descriptive or explanatory ordo amoris that accounts for variations in moral judgment.

The French reception connects this to Pascal and the “logic of the heart.” A recent French article on Scheler as a reader of Pascal notes that Scheler uses expressions such as order, logic, and mathematics of the heart to stress that the heart has laws; it also cites Ricœur’s formulation that Scheler gives phenomenological support to the objective content of higher emotional acts, distinct from ordinary emotional sensations.

For philosophy of mind, the consequence is radical: affect is not secondary to cognition. The deepest cognitive orientation of the person is affective-axiological. What a person can notice, understand, value, will, and become depends on the order of love.

9. The person: neither ego, nor psyche, nor soul-substance

Scheler’s person is one of the most important anti-reductionist notions in twentieth-century phenomenology. The person is not the empirical ego. It is not a bundle of mental states. It is not the body. It is not the psyche. It is not a soul-substance. It is not even an object of inner perception.

The person is the unity and style of acts. Scheler’s German formulation in Die Stellung is that spirit is pure actuality and has its being only in the free execution of acts; the center of spirit, the person, is not objective or thing-like being but a self-enacting order-structure of acts. The person is only in and through acts.

This is also why introspection cannot grasp the person as an object. Psychology can objectify psychic events; it can observe sensations, memories, drives, affects, and experiences. But the spiritual act that objectifies psychic life cannot itself be objectified in the same way. One can gather oneself into personal actuality, but not turn the person into a thing.

The SEP captures the same doctrine in terms of “style”: every person has a unique style of loving and assigning meaning, and the person is not reducible to subject, ego, mind, or psyche.

This gives a distinctive theory of personal identity. Personal identity is neither bodily sameness nor psychological continuity alone. It is an ordo amoris, a unique act-style, a pattern of loving, preferring, valuing, and responding to the world. The “self” is not hidden behind acts; it is actualized in them.

10. Other minds: love, sympathy, and co-execution

Scheler rejects the standard “problem of other minds” as badly posed. That problem assumes isolated minds first and then asks how one mind can infer another. Scheler starts from affective and communal life. Self-consciousness and personhood are formed within shared life, not after a solitary ego later discovers others.

The person is not given as an object the way a body is given in perception. But perception is not the only mode of givenness. The other person is given through love, sympathy, and value-comprehension. Love does not provide a list of mental contents; it gives access to who the other is and who the other could become. Scheler’s position is not mind-reading but participatory understanding.

The primary German text says the same: foreign persons, as persons, are not objectifiable; we gain knowing participation in them by co-executing their free acts, by following, understanding, and identifying ourselves with their willing and loving through spiritual love.

French early reception saw the importance of this very clearly. A 1927 article in Revue néo-scolastique de philosophie described Scheler’s work on sympathy and love as an introduction to his whole philosophy and as an analysis of personal love that grounds his religious, moral, and metaphysical philosophy.

Thus, in a Schelerian philosophy of mind, social cognition is not primarily inference from behavior to hidden mental states. It is expressive, affective, value-perceptive, and participatory. The other is not a theoretical hypothesis; the other is encountered as a person.

11. Theology is not a distraction: the holy as a dimension of mind

Scheler’s theological and religious thought must be treated as structurally internal to his philosophy of mind. There are two major phases to distinguish.

In the earlier and middle work, Scheler develops a phenomenology of religious experience. He argues that religious experience has its own evidential mode: revelation. The holy or absolute is not given by rational proof; rational proofs come too late because they already presuppose some disclosure of the divine or holy. A phenomenology of religion must therefore describe revelation according to its own mode of givenness and lawfulness.

This is not fideism in the simple sense. Scheler is not saying that religion is irrational and therefore exempt from philosophy. He is saying that modern rationalism has too narrow a concept of evidence. Religious experience, like value-experience and personal love, has a distinctive structure of givenness. That structure can be described phenomenologically.

In the later work, the theology becomes more speculative and less orthodox. Scheler links world-consciousness, self-consciousness, and God-consciousness. Once the human being becomes world-open, objectifies nature, and becomes self-conscious, it also necessarily encounters the formal idea of an absolute being marked by holiness. The sphere of absolute being belongs constitutively to the human essence, just as self-consciousness and world-consciousness do.

This is an extraordinary claim. It means that the mind’s world-openness structurally includes a metaphysical-religious horizon. To be human is not merely to represent objects or solve problems. It is to stand outside immediate environment, confront contingency, ask why there is anything at all, and orient oneself toward absolute being.

But Scheler’s late account departs from classical theism. He explicitly rejects, for philosophical purposes, the theistic premise of an omnipotent personal God. The world-ground realizes itself in the human being; the human heart and self are the accessible locus of God-becoming. God and human becoming are mutually implicated, and knowledge of absolute being becomes possible only through personal commitment, co-execution, and active identification rather than detached objectification.

The theological implication for philosophy of mind is profound. The person is not merely a finite psychological subject. The person is a site in which value, spirit, world, and divine becoming intersect. Scheler’s final metaphysics makes human personal acts responsible for the realization of higher values and, in a strong sense, for the becoming of God.

12. The extracted Schelerian philosophy of mind

A comprehensive Schelerian philosophy of mind can be stated as a set of theses.

First, mind is not identical with consciousness. The psychical begins with life itself as inwardness. Consciousness arises within life; self-consciousness arises only with spirit. This distinguishes Scheler both from Cartesianism and from any theory that treats conscious representation as the ground of mentality.

Second, body and soul are not separate substances. At the level of life, body and soul are two aspects of one psychophysical process. The body is not a machine inhabited by a soul; the soul is not an inner theater detached from the body. Psychical life is embodied life seen from within.

Third, the human mind is not defined by intelligence. Practical intelligence exists in animals and remains bound to drives and environment. Human distinctiveness lies in spirit: objectivity, essence-intuition, value-disclosure, self-consciousness, and world-openness.

Fourth, cognition is affectively grounded. The world is first encountered as meaningful, resistant, attractive, repellent, valuable, threatening, promising. Reality is given as resistance to drive before it is given as representation. Values are disclosed by feeling before they are conceptually judged.

Fifth, emotion is intentional. Higher emotions are not mere subjective states but acts that disclose values. Love and hate are not psychological ornaments placed on an already neutral world; they are movements that open or close the world of value.

Sixth, the person is an act-center and order of love. Personal identity is not reducible to bodily continuity, memory, character traits, or mental contents. The person is the non-objectifiable unity and style of acts, especially the style of loving and preferring.

Seventh, freedom is not disembodied causation. Spirit cannot create energy ex nihilo. Life provides energy; spirit directs, inhibits, releases, and sublimates it. Agency is therefore a relation of value-guided spiritual form to embodied drive-energy.

Eighth, other persons are not inferred objects. They are encountered through expressive, affective, loving, and participatory acts. Other minds are not hidden Cartesian interiors but personal worlds accessible through love and co-execution.

Ninth, theology belongs to the structure of mind. The human being’s world-openness entails the horizon of absolute being. Religious experience and revelation have their own evidential mode in the middle Scheler, while the late Scheler transforms this into a speculative metaphysics of God-becoming through human personal acts.

13. Where Scheler is strongest

Scheler is especially strong on five points.

He gives a non-Cartesian account of embodiment without collapsing mind into mechanism. Body and soul are one life seen from different sides, which allows him to avoid both substance dualism and reductive physicalism.

He gives a powerful account of affective cognition. Values are not late intellectual interpretations; they are disclosed in feeling. This makes Scheler a major resource for contemporary debates about emotion, value perception, moral psychology, and affective intentionality.

He gives a non-representational account of reality-experience. Reality is first resistance to drive, not a neutral datum inside consciousness. This can be fruitfully compared with enactivism, embodied cognition, pragmatism, and phenomenologies of affect, though Scheler’s metaphysical commitments are stronger than most contemporary versions.

He gives a rich account of personhood. The person is not the empirical ego, not the stream of consciousness, not the body, not the psyche, and not a hidden substance. The person is the act-unity, value-orientation, and order of love that becomes visible in living, acting, loving, and responding.

He takes theology and the holy seriously as modes of givenness rather than dismissing them as subjective projections. Even when his late metaphysics departs from orthodox theism, it does so because he thinks world-openness forces the human being into the question of absolute being.

14. Where Scheler is most vulnerable

The first difficulty is the relation between powerless spirit and powerful life. If spirit lacks original energy, how exactly does it inhibit, direct, or sublimate drives? Scheler’s answer is suggestive but incomplete: spirit guides through ideas, values, attention, and inhibition, while life supplies energy. But the precise causal or ontological relation remains obscure. The SEP notes that Scheler’s late dualism leaves open questions about how spirit and life interact and how the human being is unified if it participates in two irreducible movements.

The second difficulty is the instability of Geist. Guido Cusinato’s Italian scholarship emphasizes that Scheler’s concept of spirit is highly unstable and that a major shift occurs around 1923: earlier, person and spirit are tightly correlated; later, spirit becomes a broader metaphysical principle and person is one expression of it.

The third difficulty is edition-critical. Some late formulations of Geist and Drang may be affected by posthumous editorial history. Since Die Stellung is crucial for this topic, any definitive interpretation should compare the 1928 text, the 1947 reworked text, the Gesammelte Werke, and critical editions.

The fourth difficulty is scientific datedness. Scheler’s biology, psychology, and ethology should not be imported wholesale into contemporary philosophy of mind. His structural claims are far more durable than his empirical examples. The durable claims are: psychical life is embodied; consciousness is stratified; drives and affect are foundational; intelligence is not personhood; world-openness and value-disclosure distinguish spirit.

The fifth difficulty is theological tension. The middle Scheler treats revelation and the holy in ways compatible with a Christian phenomenology of religion. The late Scheler rejects an omnipotent personal creator God and speaks instead of a becoming God realized through human participation. That is not a minor adjustment; it changes the metaphysics of personhood, spirit, and divine agency.

15. A final synthetic formulation

Scheler’s philosophy of mind can be summarized as an axiological, embodied, personalist, and theological phenomenology of consciousness.

The soul is the inwardness of life, not a Cartesian substance. Consciousness is a stratified achievement within embodied life, not the first principle of philosophy. Human mind is not merely intelligence but spirit: the capacity to objectify, intuit essences, disclose values, become self-conscious, and open itself to world and absolute being. The person is the non-objectifiable unity of acts, the lived order of love, the unique style by which a world becomes meaningful. Love is not an emotion added to cognition; it is the act by which persons and values become visible. Theology is not decorative; the holy and the absolute are structurally implicated in the human capacity for world-openness and self-transcendence.

The most compact Schelerian formula would be:

Human mind is embodied life opened by spirit into a world of values, unified as person through an order of love, and oriented—whether religiously, metaphysically, or both—toward the absolute.

Digital Minds · A history of thinking about the mind, from antiquity to the present day.
A personal research project by Fabián. · About · Glossary
